text: The surgeon begins developing the preperitoneal plane for a left inguinal hernia repair. The hernia is visible on the left side, and the thin peritoneum is dissected near the back of the rectus muscle.

The surgeon dissects medially towards the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, visible in the surgical field. The dissection is in the preperitoneal plane laterally and in the pre-transverse salus or retro-rectus plane medially. The transverse salus fascia is attached to the semilunar line and is being taken down below the arcuate line to re-enter the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon sweeps the cord structures laterally and decides to abandon the hernia sac that extends towards the scrotum to avoid excessive dissection in the inguinal canal. The sac is amputated at the current level, and the surgeon continues to parietalize the area to accommodate the inferior portion of the mesh.

The surgeon displays the obturator space and creates additional room for the mesh to extend slightly across the midline. A cord lipoma is checked, and the critical view is established. The mesh is placed into the pocket and unfolded serially. The flap is closed, addressing the hole left by the amputated sac.

These cases are taking me about forty-five minutes skin to skin. There was nothing particularly challenging about this case. It certainly can be done faster with a laparoscopic TEP. I think in a young healthy individual the benefits of a speedy surgery are negligible and surgeon comfort and preference has validity. I'm a fan of the TAP approach, and I'm a fan of the robotic platform, just due to the dexterity and comfortability of the procedure. That being said, I'm very comfortable with all forms of laparoscopic, TEP, TAP, ETEP, and robotic, and will adjust based upon what the case calls for. close the defect in the sac. I do this by suspending it to the peritoneal closure.
And that concludes the procedure. I hope you enjoyed.
